dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 12 for a female with breathing problems and fever at an apartment in [CITY].
Hello, I'm Sarah, an EMT from Riverside Ambulance. What made you call 911 tonight?
Oh, I can't breathe, and I have a really high fever. It just started being a problem again a few hours ago.
I see. Can you tell me your name and the date?
My name is Michelle. I'm not sure about the date-it's either the 10th or the 11th, maybe?
Can you tell me which city you're in right now?
I'm in [CITY]. I, uh, I'm in [CITY].
We're going to do a quick assessment. Do you mind if we check your airway?
Ma'am, your breaths seem to come in fast and deep. May we listen to your lungs?
Let me take your pulse. It feels fast. Are you experiencing any shaking or tremors?
Great. You're aware of who you are and where you are, the time, and the events that have just transpired.
Your airway is clear and you can speak clearly, so it seems patent and maintainable.
There are no obstructions or sounds from your airway indicating difficulty breathing.
Your breathing rate is 22. Is it consistently this fast?
Your breathing appears labored-your nostrils are flaring and you're breathing deeply.
I hear wheezing, especially on the lower part of your lung area.
Your skin appears warm and dry, with no signs of cyanosis yet.
Your heart rate is high-about 111 beats per minute. Your skin feels normal.
Skin is warm, not cold or clammy. No signs of poor circulation at this time.
Can you tell me a bit more about how you're feeling overall?
I started feeling sick yesterday afternoon-chills, vomiting, and a very bad fever. I thought I was getting better after a nap, then this morning I woke up with an asthma attack.
You mentioned asthma. Is there anything else we should know about your medical history?
Yeah, I have chronic migraines and kidney disease. It's stage 1-they told me it was not too bad.
Are you currently taking any medications on a regular basis?
Yes-almotriptan for migraines, loratadine, Singulair, fenofibrate (Tricor), and topiramate (Topamax).
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate the difficulty you're having breathing?
It's not pain but more of an 8-really hard to catch a full breath.
I notice you're diaphoretic. Do you have any unusual pain, swelling, or redness in your mouth or throat?
Not right now-except, maybe I feel nauseated, but I haven't vomited since EMS arrived.
Your lower lungs are wheezy, but after a few Neb treatments, they've improved. Have you experienced any other breathing-related discomfort?
I feel like my body is on high alert, and my head still aches from the migraine.
Heart rate is 111-faster than normal. That's common when people are short of breath.
Your breathing is 22 breaths per minute. Any history of asthma exacerbations?
Your blood pressure is 119 over 72-looks stable.
Your oxygen saturation is at 94%-a little low but within acceptable range.
Your heart rhythm is sinus tachycardia now. We'll continue monitoring and keep the four-lead ECG on you.
Your blood sugar is 136-normal and stable. We'll keep checking it.
I'm going to give you a DuoNeb treatment-combination of albuterol and Atrovent. This should help open your airways.
The nebulizer is going to deliver 5 milligrams of albuterol and 0.5 milligrams ipratropium. Breathe deeply through the mask when it's ready.
We also have you on 8 liters per minute oxygen. You should notice some improvement in a few minutes.
I'll also put you on a four-lead cardiac monitor just to make sure everything's stable and safe. Do you feel up for the monitor?
We are initiating protocol for asthma exacerbation and respiratory distress per standing order.
Pulse is 115 now-still up, but not dramatically. Is your breathing stable or getting better?
Respiratory rate is 20 per minute now-still a bit high, but better. Any improvement in your breathing sensation?
Blood pressure is 114/88-good. Have you had any reactions to any medications lately, maybe from something you took or ate?
Your blood oxygen has improved to 99%, which is great.
No change in rhythm-still showing sinus tachycardia.
Taking a quick glucose test now. You might feel a quick pinch on your finger.
Earlier you mentioned migraines. Has the migraine improved or still active?
It's improving slowly. It was so severe all day. The migraine has been a constant thing for me for years now.
You are still A&O to person, place, time, and events.
What about allergies? Do you have any that affect the medications you usually take?
I'm allergic to doxycycline, Fioricet, and Percocet.
During the vomiting episode you mentioned, did you lose any fluids or pass anything out before we arrived?
I vomited once, but not enough to feel dehydrated. I also had a slight cold sweat, maybe from fever.
Your GCS is 15, which means you are fully awake and oriented with a good neurological response.
I attempted to start an IV in your arm for fluids and access-it will use a small 18-gauge needle. Unfortunately, it did not go in successfully, but we'll maintain a saline lock for future use.
You're going to be taken to [COMPANY_NAME]. There's a lot of experience there for asthma and migraines. Is that okay with you?
We'll now lower you from your bed to the stair chair and gently transport you to the back of the ambulance in Fowler's position.
Your peripheral pulses are equal, regular, and strong.
Things are stable, and all monitoring shows improvement. Your blood glucose is 136-normal.
You appear to be slightly tremulous and hyperventilating, but not in acute distress.
You are not in pain at this time-pain level 0.
The care of the patient has been transferred to the receiving facility staff.
